content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To : Susan
Advice Files 87200, 87300

Date : Jan. 27, 1982

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
Diane

Subject: Disclosure Obligations of Planning Commissioners
and Variations on the Theme

Section 87200 provides that planning commissioners are
Article II filers. However, we have discovered that there are
several types of planning commissions created under the Government Code. In this memo, I will discuss the authority and
functions of the various types of planning commissions, and the
applicable disclosure requirements for each one.

ARTICLE II FILERS:

1. Section 65100(a) Planning Commissions. These are the
traditional, typical planning commissions set up by a city or
county. Members of these commissions are Article II filers who
file with the Commission.

2. Section 65100(b) Planning Commissions. This section
authorizes a city or county to set up two planning commissions
in lieu of one commission under Section 65100(a). One of the
Commissions does long-range planning for the jurisdiction, and
the other meets the short-range, day-to-day needs of the area.
Since their role is the same, albeit divided, as that of 65100(a)
commissions, these commissioners are also Article II filers.

3. Section 65100(c) Area Planning Commissions within One
County. This section permits a county to designate planning
commissions for specified areas of the county. Each commission
operates autonomously within its area. These commissioners are
thus also Article II filers.

CODE FILERS:

4. Section 65600 Area Planning Commissions. This section
allows cities and counties to establish a joint area planning
commission by resolution. The functions of the commission are
dictated by a plan which is mutually agreed upon by the cooperating

Susan
Advice Files 87200, 87300
January 27, 1982
Page Two


cities and counties. Since the duties and powers of these
commissions may vary widely depending on the plan, these
commissioners should have their disclosure responsibilities
spelled out in a conflict of interest code. More often than
not, the disclosure should be Article II type if they are
making substantive decisions and recommendations.

     5. Section 66100 Area Planning Districts. Two or more
counties may join together to create a planning district
under this section by board resolution. The statute states
that the purpose of such a district shall be to develop a
long-term general plan for the physical development of the
district which would include findings, a statement of the
problems, and recommendations regarding the future of the
district. Each district is a public agency with broad powers
and duties spelled out in the statutes. The governing board
members must be county supervisors, city mayors and/or council
members. These districts should have conflict of interest
codes which assign Article II type disclosure to the board
members.

     6. Planning Commissions Organized as Joint Powers
Agencies Under Section 6500. Section 6500 permits local
entities to agree to perform a governmental function or
service as a joint venture. Some of these joint powers agencies
are organized to provide planning service or guidance in an
area. Examples of these agencies are ABAG, SCAG, and IMAGE.
The powers and duties of these agencies are dictated by the
joint powers agreement. These agencies should have conflict
of interest codes and, if similar to an agency such as ABAG,
should assign Article II type disclosure to the board members.

DMF:km
